data.title: Banco Bradesco Deepens Its Recovery, But The Market Still Waits
data.description:
Since my last update, Banco Bradesco (BBD) not only maintained its operational improvement path, but in Q2 2025 it managed to deepen several of the advances that I already anticipated.

The bank again showed solidity in its main metrics: recurring net profit reached R$6.1B, with an ROAE that rose to 14.6% and a credit growth that remained above the sector average. The profitability, efficiency, and quality of the portfolio continue to recover, while the expansion of the digital channel and new technological developments consolidate its competitive profile.

Despite this progress, BBD’s stock continues to operate with depressed multiples, partly due to the persistent market caution in the face of challenges imposed by the Brazilian context and the pressure of new digital players. Today the valuation discount is even more pronounced than in previous quarters, although fundamentals show a clear improvement.

For all this, I consider that the best option remains "hold": the results of Q2 reinforce the value thesis, but the catalyst for revaluation still depends on clearer signals on the macro and competitive front.

Overview

Bradesco (BBD) is going through 2025 as a bank that has been able to convert itself amid the technological pressure and transformation of the Brazilian financial system. Unlike other major players, the entity has opted for a balanced approach: it maintains its physical capillarity and its historical territorial roots, but advances in the deployment of digital solutions and the improvement of the customer experience, integrating technology and AI in virtually all its services.

In Q2, the bank reinforced its strategy of combining prudent loan origination with greater operational efficiency and a clear focus on high value-added segments such as individuals and SMEs. The expansion of the portfolio, with particular vigour in personal loans, real estate, and collateralized loans, was accompanied by controlled default and robust coverage ratios, while the weight of insured loans continued to grow within the total portfolio.

The digital channel, led by the Next ecosystem and the development of new platforms for companies, was consolidated as a key engine in both customer acquisition and revenue generation and commissions. At the same time, the physical network continues to play a strategic role in areas where financial inclusion still depends on face-to-face contact and human counselling.

BBD’s current model is based on the strength of its balance sheet, the strength of its insurance franchise, and an active dividend distribution policy, factors that allow it to navigate the ups and downs of the Brazilian economy with resilience.

Q2 2025

During Q2 2025, BBD consolidated its improvement trend and showed significant progress on almost all operational and financial fronts. BBD presented a net profit of R$6.1B (+28.6% YoY) and ROAE was at 14.6%.

Revenues were R$34B (+15.1% YoY), with expansions in all lines. The net financial margin amounted to R$18.044B (+15.8% YoY), while net interest income from clients was R$17.756B (+16.4% YoY). The average spread on the portfolio was 8.8%, showing a better combination of products and efficiency in anchoring.

The expanded credit portfolio for the first time exceeded the real trillion (R$1.018T), showing an 11.7% YoY advance. Growth was particularly robust in the segments of individuals (+15.9% YoY, up to R$442.4B) and SMEs (+25.2% YoY), while large companies remained practically stable. There was also an expansion in personal loans (+17.5% YoY), mortgage credits (+17.6% YoY), and rural credits (+46.3% YoY).

Portfolio quality remained under control, with late payments over 90 days remaining at 4.1%. The provisioning ratio largely covers problematic exposure, with levels above 100% on the at-risk portfolio. In addition, the restructured portfolio decreased by 21% YoY and now represents only 4% of the total portfolio, a clear sign of improvement in credit quality.

Operating revenue from commissions and fees grew by 10.6% YoY, reaching R$10,307B. The card business was highlighted, which increased its revenue by 19.9% YoY and reached a record R$4,460b, while revenues from consortia rose 20.8% and asset management 3.7%. The insurance segment delivered a net profit of R$2.3 billion (+4.4% YoY) and an ROAE of 21.4%.

Total operating expenses amounted to R$15,89b (+9.9%YoY), explained in part by higher investments in digital transformation and the impact of new consolidations. However, personnel and administrative expenses, which are the core of the operating cost, only grew by 4.9% YoY, below inflation, showing a firm control on recurring spending.

On the equity side, BBD closed Q2 with a Tier 1 capital ratio of 13%, while dividend policy remained aggressive, bringing the annualized dividend yield to 7.8%, more than double the industry average.

Valuation, Growth, And Profitability: An Asymmetry Difficult To Ignore

The current situation of BBD reveals a rare paradox in the financial system of LatAm: a bank that exhibits growth and profitability far above its peers, but which continues to quote at discount prices, as if the market ignores the progress of its balance sheet.

BBD trades at a P/E FWD of 6.70x, almost 38% under the industry average (10.79x) and well below its 5-year average (8.05x). In addition, the PEG FWD is 0.38x, so the share price doesn’t reflect the real and projected growth of earnings, moreover, the PEG of BBD is more than 69% lower than that of the sector (1.26x) and almost 40% below its own historical avg (0.62x), which shows us that the market discount is a more negative scenario than what the balance sheets show.

The P/B FWD is 0.97x, below both sector (1.14x) and its own history (1.12x). Meanwhile, the FWD P/S is down to 1.25x, less than half of the industry average (2.79x) and more than 47% below its own average.

This disconnect is most clear when we see that BBD currently pays a dividend yield of 7.83%, more than double the industry (3.05%) and also its own 5-year avg (3.61%). This means that, in addition to the multiple appreciation potential, BBD rewards the shareholder with CF, which is very relevant in the current context of positive real rates and regional volatility.

On the growth side, the difference with the sector is even more marked. In the last 12 months, BBD’s revenues grew 23.6% YoY vs 7.2% YoY of the sector, with an EPS growth of 44.9% (vs. sector: 10.6%). Projections remain strong: the estimated growth of EPS FWD is 14.7%, 50% higher than the sectoral avg and almost three times its own 5-year avg (5.2%). The ROE TTM is 11.14%, just below its historical level but still above the sector (10.56%), and BBD’s net margin remains robust at 22.3%.

Conclusion

BBD again exceeded expectations with another strong quarter in growth and profitability. The bank continues to improve where it imports and pays a dividend that is difficult to match, but the market still does not recognize it and the stock remains cheap.

Therefore, I still believe that it is best to maintain the position: the results accompany and, meanwhile, it is possible to collect a good flow, hoping that the market will finally value all this progress.

data.title: ArcelorMittal: Upgrading My Price Target After 12 Months
data.description:
It's been about 1 year since I last invested in and also covered steel company ArcelorMittal (NYSE:MT). It was never my biggest steel or basic materials investment, preferring companies like Norsk Hydro (OTCQX:NHYDY) instead, but it was still quite excellent for what it represented. While I missed out on an early exit in early 2025, when the company for a short time "peaked", I am looking at the company after a more than 40% RoR, inclusive of dividends. This article will explain my reasoning behind this new look, and what turns out to be a raising of my price target.

If you follow my work, you will note that I have been reinvesting and selling off several of my "winners" over the past weeks.

This is somewhat contrary to being a long-term investor, but I've been moving away, year by year, from being a buy-and-hold (forever) investor. I believe the investment style that suits me, and my approach the best, is more of an analysis-enter-analysis-exit approach, with a typical 40-200% RoR target (and usually no more than that - the investments I make typically don't have it in them to give 1000% or above).

My objective with ArcelorMittal was to beat the market. This objective was achieved with a very good margin, as you can see below. The question now is whether further market-beating returns are possible after the latest results.

Because now comes the "fun" part of that cycle - considering whether to take home the "bacon" and deciding where to go next - and also, in this case, explaining my logic to you.

Let's get started.

ArcelorMittal - Recalibrating targets after 12 months time

As I am writing this article, it's the 1st of August. This is a very fitting time, since it's almost to the date 12 months ago that I wrote my last article on ArcelorMittal. Steel and basic materials are notoriously volatile a segment to invest in, and the last 12 months have been no exception to what I consider in this case to be a rule.

The company remains, with a 40+ year history, one of the premier integrated steel/mining businesses on the globe. Focusing on steelmaking plants in 15 countries, I believe at any cheap time, this company can become a "BUY". My view on the steel industry can also be seen in my quite recent article on German steel company thyssenkrupp (OTCPK:TKAMY).

However, this company is in a very different situation. So, despite "selling both", I believe they have very different profiles. ArcelorMittal's US-based mining and sourcing operations are limited (but growing, with full ownership now as of 2Q25 in Calvert). Instead, its activities are focused on areas such as South America, Europe/Eastern Europe, Central America, Africa, India, and Canada. This is both a positive and a negative. It means that the exposure to tariffs is there, but it also means that the lack of correlation to the US-based market makes it a diversification away from other steel companies, in the same way that thyssenkrupp is a play in part on Europe.

ArcelorMittal has over 120,000 people working for it as well. My reason for investing in the company is, without a doubt, in part this geographical diversification, but also full integration. In some ways, it's a much bigger version of Swedish steel company SSAB, yet it has not rushed headlong into sustainable steel production (which I view as a significant risk).

This article is something I have waited on as well, because I wanted to wait for the 2Q25 results - they came in yesterday. Much like thyssenkrupp, the picture presented is "good, but volatile". The company's margins are growing, but so are the geopolitical and cyclical challenges. MT is selling at an EBITDA of $125 per tonne, compared to an average of below $90/tonne.

This showcases a significant operational improvement, owing in no small part to its integrated business model. However, part of its share price resilience and growth has no doubt been due to the company's continuing buybacks, which have been ongoing for years. It has added over $18/share back to the book value, because MT has bought back a staggering 38% of shares outstanding during that time. As of the 2Q25 period, and as of July 31st, 2025, it means that the book value stands at $72/share compared to a share price of only roughly $32/share for the ADR MT.

MT is a much more "working" company than thyssenkrupp is. The company's fundamentals are BBB rated, and debt is extremely low, less than 20% long-term debt to capital.

The company's other advantages for the 2Q25 period include record shipments from some of its volatile geographies (Liberia, for instance), while like thyssenkrupp expects advantages from the EU's trade policy adjustments and anti-dumping duties. That being said, the trend for pricing and for the market overall is somewhat challenged. Why?

Because China's steel industry remains at a significant overcapacity, and the nation's export of steel continues. Most of the near-term market fundamentals are about tariffs and trade balances - and like I spoke of in my thyssenkrupp article, the future for the steel industry seems to move to a more regionalized production model, as opposed to globalized trends, which actually works in favor of a company such as ArcelorMittal.

North America is a growth segment, and the European segment, in my view, is bound to see significant growth and positive impact from elevated spending for defense and infrastructure. The company's EU-based operations are well-suited for this, and this means that in Europe, there are significant advantages to the company.

The time for investing in risky projects seems to be past. The focus for most companies in the field seems to be safe, high-quality growth or M&A projects with baked-in positives such as profitability and cash flow from a very early stage. The number of such projects is, of course, low. However, the company's legacy somewhat riskier projects continue to show profitability and "pay dividends", as it were.

Given the company's geographical set of exposures, the risk for this company is non-trivial. Its Brazilian operations are subject to what I can only consider to be risky trends given the current tariff situation, but the company is diversified enough to possibly "plan away" from this. The recent upgrade from a BBB- to a BBB also reflects the company's resilience, not only in the eyes of the market but in the eyes of the credit market as well.

I would say that ArcelorMittal is one of the world's most well-diversified and attractive global steel producers at this time, and while all of these companies come with their own sets of risks, the risks for this one are some we're able to actually see rather well.

Upsides for the company include its significant ongoing growth projects, which are set to make it a player to be reckoned with in the US, including its new Alabama asset, as well as a JV stake in Vallourec to increase the company's margins. The recovery I forecasted in my last article has in part happened, but more upside is possible from a mixed European steel market and demand rebound, more M&A's, and better efficiencies. At least, that is what I can see on the upside side. I say that 2Q25, despite all of the ongoing macro challenges, was a good quarter that justifies taking a careful look at my assumptions and price target, and updating them accordingly.

ArcelorMittal - Raising my PT

In my last article on ArcelorMittal, I gave the company a $40/share PT, based on a fair value for the company of roughly $60/share. I'm raising that as of this time. The company is expected to grow 15-16% per year at the very least for the next 3 years. Furthermore, the 1.48% yield doesn't scare me away - mining and basic materials do not tend to have overly large yields.

But the upside, even now, is non-trivial. The valuation trends are confirmed by the Seeking Alpha Quant ratings model, which puts the company at no less than a B+ or higher in every single part, despite, at the time of my last article, being more of a "HOLD".

Arguments for rotation or selling at this time are that the company is fully priced if you want to take very low or no risks at all, especially if compared to peers like Nucor (NUE) and Steel Dynamics (STLD).

I disagree with that assessment. I believe the combination of geographical diversification, of superb operating track record, and of the massive reversal and growth potential that the company has on a forecast basis, that this company is not only better valued at an average weighted P/E of less than 9.1x, it has significantly better upside. I therefore believe that it is not the time for rotation of ArcelorMittal - something I would consider if the company breached perhaps my PT of $45/share.

I will forecast MT at a P/E of 14x, the 5-year average, and based on that and current growth estimates, we have yet another triple-digit RoR potential.

Even if we discount the company for its inability to hit targets (they mostly miss or hit), you could discount it severely and still get a 15% annualized upside or above. And on what basis are we discounting these estimates? I believe the exposure to Europe, the JVs, and the growth projects will materialize positively. It would take a significant headwind to put a roadblock in the path of this company's trends, and any roadblock like that is very likely to affect competitors, like NUE and STLD, and others as well, if perhaps not to the same extent.

The simple fact is, on the basis of earnings forecast, ArcelorMittal remains significantly undervalued, and in fact cheaper than its competition, except for high-risk plays such as thyssenkrupp.

Next to peers such as Nucor, MT compares very favorably. Nucor trades at an average weighted P/E of 17.25x, which is close to double that of MT. Even if we forecast at a 14x P/E, similar level, this company/peer does not hit 15% annualized, and at the 5-year average is in fact below 10% annualized.

And Nucor also misses its estimates and targets most of the time. So on a peer basis, this and other companies are not, in my view, more attractive than ArcelorMittal. I, in fact, believe the upside of ArcelorMittal and its diversification to be far more compelling in an investment here.

Analyst targets would concur. The native ticker MT trades at €27.5/share, with an average 13-analyst price target of €35.8/share, an upside of 14% at this time. Out of 13 analysts, 8 are at "BUY" or "outperform" for the company, indicating a decent amount of conviction here.

Based on this, and the fact that this represents about 50% of the company's recorded book value, I would say that ArcelorMittal is a "BUY".

I would therefore adjust my fair value target to be at least $65/share, up from $60, to account for buybacks, and for the PT to move from $40 to $45/share, making the company a "BUY" with a 15% annualized upside, even to something like a 12-13x P/E here.

Risks to my positive, continued thesis on the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Tariff risks are by far the largest to consider. It's not an invalid argument to say that we need to wait for the full impacts to be able to be calculated. Secondary risks, due also to its EU exposure, are its lack of ESG/sustainability development. ArcelorMittal is still mostly legacy, unlike companies like SSAB, and the cost to shift this, if this becomes too costly, will be massive. This is as of yet not included in the company's plans.

Beyond that, we're mostly talking historical correlations, such as MT being a historical underperformer - I believe the current situation rises above this.

My updated PT is therefore $45/share for the ADR MT.

Thesis

  • My updated thesis for ArcelorMittal is currently a positive one for one of the leaders in global steel. I view the company as investable on the basis of its sector-leading low debt and coverage and decent upside, even after 2Q25.
  • The company's recent performance details not only operational improvements and good upside based on reversal, as well as new growth projects/JVs, but also higher upside due to a high book value thanks to share buybacks.
  • Based on this, I retain my "BUY" rating, and I'm planning to add to my current position in the business. I am further upgrading my PT to $45/share.
  • My PT for MT is upgraded to $45/share, with the company currently trading at less than $32/share as of August 2025.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $45/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a “Buy rating as of August 2025.

data.title: Radian Group: Buyback And Solid Results Make Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares of Radian Group (RDN) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 7% of their value. While the private mortgage insurer has delivered excellent financial results, this has been priced in, and focus has shifted to the potential for earnings to decline as losses eventually normalize. Indeed, Q2 results were strong, but they have done little to meaningfully alter sentiment. Last covered shares in May, rating Radian a “buy.” Shares reached my $37 target, though they have since given back all of those gains. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Radian.

In the company’s second quarter, Radian Group earned $1.01, which beat estimates by $0.03 as revenue was basically flat to last year at $318 million. Earnings were flat to last year. However, I would note that results were boosted by a $5 million tax benefit. Otherwise, the quarter was essentially in-line with analyst estimates. Unlike peers, MGIC Investment Corp. (MTG), which reported a large beat, RDN is taking a more cautious approach to its reserves, causing earnings to be a bit lower.

RDN now has $277 billion of insurance in force, up about $4 billion from a year ago. The company generated $234 million of earned premiums, consistent with prior quarters. While the company is growing its insurance pool, there has been some pricing compression. RDN had a 38.1bp portfolio yield, flat sequentially and down 0.4bps from last year. With the mortgage insurance industry being incredibly profitable over the past four years, new capital has entered the segment, pressuring pricing to a degree.

Importantly, RDN is maintaining discipline, and I prefer its approach of maintaining portfolio quality, rather than chasing growth, especially given the tenuous positioning of the housing market currently. In Q2, Raidan wrote $14.3 billion of new policies, up slightly from $13.9 billion last year. Only 4.8% of borrowers had a FICO score below 680, down slightly from 5.1% a year ago. 16.7% of loans have a loan-to-value above 95%. Its existing policies have 20% 95%+ LTVs at origination (with ongoing monthly payments, this share is now substantially lower vs. at origination). This ~3.5% improvement speaks to RDN tightening underwriting given stretched housing affordability and softening prices.

As I have discussed in the past, I like to separate Radian’s exposure into two buckets. I view policies written in 2021 and earlier as having nearly no risk. Radian provides insurance to homeowners who cannot afford a 20% down payment. It faces losses when a borrower defaults and when the home is worth less than the remaining mortgage balance. Strong employment has limited defaults, and then given how much home prices have risen, most borrowers have so much equity that they can sell their house for more than the mortgage balance.

Now with each policy that Radian writes, the backbook’s share of the total business shrinks. Still, 2021 and earlier policies account for 41% of the portfolio. While 2022 is not a no risk pool, there is also substantial equity underpinning many of these loans. Because such a substantial portion of the portfolio is written before 2022, I expect loss rates to remain very low. Plus, given elevated rates today, refinancing activity is low, allowing a strong ~84% persistency in the portfolio.

I also think it is important to emphasize that while the housing market is softening, pricing is still flattish nationally. As such, we are not likely to see a pronounced surge in negative equity homes. That said, we are seeing prices soften, particularly in the Sun Belt, where there has been more construction activity, creating supply gluts. I expect mortgage rates to broadly remain in the 6.5-7.25% area, which will keep affordability pressures. My base case is for a 0-3% decline in home prices over the next year, though there is likely to be a weaker performance in states like Florida.

By definition, recent policies are more susceptible to home price declines as they were purchased at current market levels. As such, we are seeing the company take a fairly cautious approach to reserves for losses. In Q2, Radian added $12 million to reserves. However, I would note that inside this $12 million, it set aside $48 million for new policies, which was offset by $36 million of favorable developments in prior years. It continues to have excess legacy reserves because of the low defaults for 2021 and earlier that I noted above. Indeed, Radian has been achieving a 98% cure rate on 2021-2022 defaults, validating my view that these policies are nearly no loss.

The overall portfolio continues to perform solidly. RDN-insured borrowers have a 2.27% default rate, down 6 bps sequentially but up from 2.04% last year. The company’s decision to add reserves, which now total $383 million, reflects more conservatism about future policies, after largely holding reserves flat last year. These reserves should help to insulate profitability in the event we do see a moderate downturn in housing, and if home prices actually continue to rise, the company is likely positioned to release reserves next year. Overall, I view RDN’s reserving policy as more indicative of management’s general conservatism than a warning sign of an impending problem.

Elsewhere, RDN has a $6.5 billion investment portfolio, down $100 million from last year, as it returns capital to investors. Because the portfolio is slightly smaller, it generated $73 million of net investment income (“NII”), down $1 million from last year. This is a high-quality portfolio. Radian only owns $20 million of below-investment-grade rated bonds, and the majority of the portfolio is AA-rated or higher. Much of the portfolio is in mortgages and asset-backed securities that gradually amortize. I view fixed-rate reinvestment as likely to only be a modest tailwind to results.

Radian has a strong capital position, including $2 billion of excess available assets, though this was down $100 million sequentially due to capital returns. RDN also has a 19.2% debt to capital ratio, which I view as healthy. It has sent $400 million of dividends from the operating company to the holding company so far this year. It remains on track to send $795 million this year.

These intercompany flows are critical because it is the holding company that pays the common dividend and repurchases stock. As a result, RDN has $784 million of holding company liquidity, leaving it positioned for ongoing capital returns. It pays a 3.1% dividend yield, and in Q2, it did $223 million of repurchases. As a result, its share count is down over 10% from last year. I expect at least $350 million of H2 buybacks and more likely $400+ million.

Radian is well positioned to continue sending dividends to the holding company. As you can see below, with each year, about $450-500 million of capital is released. Now, as it writes new policies, it will need to set aside capital. Still, given the pace of roll-off, RDN should be able to dividend up at least 100% of net income in the coming years, which can support buybacks and a growing dividend.

Excluding its $273 million AOCI loss, the book value is $35.20. Given RDN’s conservative approach to reserves, I continue to believe shares should trade at least at book value. Back in May, I saw the company earning $3.85-$4.05. With Q2 results in hand, I now see the company earning $3.90-$4.05. As its backbook continues to shrink, I see run-rate EPS of closer to $3.70. After this quarter, I continue to see $37 as Radian’s fair value or 10x run-rate, and I believe the recent pullback has created an attractive opportunity. I am a buyer.

data.title: Barrick Mining: Stock Performance To Benefit From Sound Strategies
data.description:
Barrick Mining (NYSE:B) has multiple positive factors in its favor that could drive the stock to outperform over the next year or two. The stock has a reasonable valuation, leaving room to the upside as the company continues to grow. Barrick's above-average double-digit expected revenue and earnings growth should be the positive driver for the stock. Barrick has multiple strategies to drive strong long-term growth.

Barrick Mining's Company Background

Barrick operates as a Canada-based $37 billion market cap gold and copper miner. The company's strategy is to plan for long-term global exploration programs, designed to deliver a steady stream of new business opportunities. The company is focused primarily on growing organically through exploration instead of through acquisitions. Barrick Mining changed its name from Barrick Gold earlier this year.

Barrick Mining has 89 million ounces of proven and probable mineral reserves as of the end of 2024. The company grew this significantly from 77 million ounces as of the end of 2023. This was accomplished by the conversion of the Reko Diq copper-gold resources to mineral reserves in Pakistan. This conversion added 13 million ounces of gold and 7.3 million tonnes of copper. Barrick owns 50% of Reko Diq, which is one of the largest undeveloped copper-gold projects in the world. Reko Diq is currently under construction. The first production from Reko Diq is expected to be at the end of 2028.

In North America, the company operates the Nevada Gold mines: Carlin, Cortex, and Turquoise Ridge. Those three mines produced 342,000 ounces of gold in Q1 2025. Also in North America is the Fourmile mine which has a 2025 program of 16 drilling rigs, drilling 73 holes totaling 55km. This action at Fourmile is to get the mine to pre-feasibility.

In Canada, Barrick is doing generative work in the Southern Superior region to characterize fertility of different greenstone belts. The company is also has active exploration efforts underway in the Northern Cordillera and the Appalachia regions of Canada. Barrick sees 22 advanced opportunities with over 40 early-staged projects reviewed in Canada.

The company operates the Pueblo Viejo mine in the Dominican Republic. Pueblo Viejo has a plant expansion and mine life extension plan designed to increase throughput to 14 metric tons and to sustain gold production of over 800,000 ounces per year. This mine produced 74,000 ounces of gold in Q1 2025.

Barrick's Kabili gold mine in the Congo had lower gold production of 63k ounces of gold in Q1 2025 vs. 76k ounces in Q1 2024 due to planned lower grades processed through the plant. However, operational improvements and continued investments are expected to lead to better results in the 2nd half of 2025.

In Tanzania, Barrick operates the North Mara mine which produced 67k ounces of gold in Q1 2025 and the Bulyanhulu mine which produced 37k ounces of gold in Q1 2025. The company is making operational improvements in both at both of these mines.

The Lumwana mine in Zambia produced 27k ounces of copper in Q1 2025. This was up from the 22k ounces produced in Q1 2024, but down significantly from the record of 46k ounces produced in Q4 2024. Production decreased from Q4 due to lower grades. However, higher grades are expected to be fed beginning in Q2.

Growth Catalysts

Barrick has multiple catalysts to drive long-term growth. The company has sound strategies to drive this growth. Barrick's focus on Tier One gold assets and growing the copper portfolio can help provide the company with long-term profitable growth. Tier One gold assets have the potential to deliver a minimum of a 10-year life of production of over 500,000 ounces per year and are in the lower half of the industry cost curve. Tier One copper assets have a reserve potential of more than 5 million tonnes and are in the lower half of the industry cost curve. So, this strategy can help drive Barrick Mining's production while keeping costs contained.

Another strategy includes Barrick's effort to maintain a disciplined reserve and resource replacement. This involves long-term planning for ongoing organic growth. This is a more cost-effective strategy for long-term growth as an alternative to growing through acquisitions which are typically much more expensive.

Barrick also employs strict discipline to find quality growth assets that meet the company's conservative investment criteria. This strategy involves not taking on new debt and not diluting shareholders. Barrick's strategy is to increase its per share value through cash investments. These investments are sustained by Barrick's existing businesses.

Barrick has a strategy that has industry-leading global exploration programs. This enables the company to find new growth opportunities in areas throughout the world containing high-quality metals.

The company strives to maintain a strong balance sheet. Barrick has $4.1 billion in total cash and $4.7 billion in total debt leaving the company with net debt of $623 million. That's not a bad amount of net debt for a capital intensive business. Barrick has 2.9x more current assets than current liabilities and 3.3x more total assets than total liabilities for total equity of $33.6 billion. Barrick's largest assets are its $28.7 billion in property, plant, & equipment. The largest liabilities after long-term debt are $3.8 billion in deferred tax liability and $3.2 billion in other non-current liabilities.

Barrick stated in the Q1 earnings conference call that the company would pay a special dividend when net debt reaches zero to a positive $500 million in cash. That is something that investors should keep in mind since Barrick is only $600 million short of cash to hit zero net debt.

Barrick's strong cash position and cash flow gives the company the flexibility of growing the business organically which is a part of their strategy. The company had a trailing 12 month [TTM] operating cash flow of $4.9 billion. Over the TTM period, Barrick paid $693 million in dividends, repurchased $641 million of stock, and spent $3.2 billion in CapEx, leaving the company with unlevered free cash flow of $1.4 billion.

The strength in the price of gold is a positive catalyst for Barrick. Gold has remained above $3,000 per ounce over the past few months. According to JPMorgan (JPM), the price of gold is expected to continue higher with an average price of $3,675/oz. by Q4 2025. The strong price of gold ensures that Barrick will get a strong revenue boost from each ounce that is sold.

Valuation

Barrick Mining currently has a net asset value of $19.5 (total assets of 48.142 billion - total liabilities of $14.578 billion)/total outstanding shares of 1719.5. Barrick is trading slightly higher with a current stock price of $21.12. The stock looks reasonably valued from a NAV perspective.

To get another perspective, Barrick is trading at 10.5x expected EPS of $2.02 for 2025. This is below the sector median of 15.4. Barrick's forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 5.16 as compared to the sector median of 8.47. From this perspective, the stock looks undervalued.

I would put more weight on the P/NAV since that is the main valuation metric for miners. Investors might consider buying the stock whenever the price drops below the NAV. This can ensure that you are getting the stock at a great value.

Technical Perspective

Barrick's weekly chart above shows the volatile nature of the stock over a multiple-year period. The RSI (purple line in the middle of the chart) is still in bullish territory as it remains above the 50 level. The MACD (bottom of the chart) is also bullish as the blue MACD line remains above the red signal line and the histogram bars are in the positive green zone.

It is important to point out the volatile nature of the stock. The chart does show the large fluctuations in the price of over 20% within just a few months in both directions. Investors can take advantage of these swings by buying the dips.

It is possible that the stock dips again from here which it tends to do when the RSI moves above 60. The best buying opportunities were when the RSI dropped to about 40 or below. The buying opportunities can be crossed-checked with the Price/NAV valuation. Buy when the stock price falls below the NAV (when the Price/NAV drops below zero).

The next likely catalyst for the stock is the next earnings report scheduled for August 11, 2025 for Q2. Analysts are expecting Q2 revenue of $3.7 billion and normalized EPS of $0.46. I would expect the report to be positive given the strong price of gold which should help to boost revenue. Plus, revenue and earnings estimates have been revised upwards over the past 6 months.

Risks for the Stock

Barrick's stock tends to be highly correlated to the price of gold. Sharp drops in the price of gold can lead to significant declines for the stock. Lower gold prices mean that Barrick will earn less revenue from each ounce that it sells. The price of gold has been strong so far in 2025. However, there is no guarantee that the positive trend will continue. The company and stock can also be negatively impacted by declines in the price of copper.

The company is at risk of government actions in the regions where it operates. Governments could impose higher taxes or other regulations which could make doing business more expensive in those countries. Barrick needs to maintain positive partnerships among the countries that it operates in to prevent unfavorable actions for the company.

Barrick is at risk of catastrophic natural disasters in the areas where it operates. Natural disasters could lead to significant delays and unexpected costs associated with repairing/replacing the loss of equipment and returning to normal operations.

Barrick Mining: Long-Term Outlook

Barrick appears to have a sound multi-pronged strategy for long-term growth. The company's focus on Tier One gold assets and growing the copper portfolio should help drive strong growth, while keeping costs contained. Barrick's focus on resource replacement and organic growth is another key strategy that gives the company a competitive edge for long-term growth.

I like how the company effectively manages its balance sheet to strive for zero net debt. Barrick's asset-heavy balance sheet should provide protection from downturns as the company has the flexibility to sell assets if needed.

With a reasonable valuation from a NAV perspective, the stock has a chance to outperform the broader market due to Barrick's above average expected growth. Barrick is expected to grow revenue at 18% in 2025 and follow this up with another 18% growth in 2026 according to analysts' estimates. This would significantly exceed analysts' expectations for the S&P 500 (SPY) revenue growth of 5.3% for 2025 and 6.3% for 2026.

Barrick's above average expected earnings growth of 60% for 2025 and 25% for 2026 significantly exceeds analysts' expectations for the S&P 500 of 9.6% earnings growth for 2025 and 13.9% for 2026. Barrick's strategies have the likely potential to drive performance to meet or exceeds its estimates.

data.title: Brookfield Infrastructure: Recession Cancelled, 5.5% Dividend Yield, Investment-Grade Rating Affirmed
data.description:
Brookfield Infrastructure (BIP) (BIPC) is a buy for long-term dividend growth built on the back of a global portfolio of mission-critical infrastructure assets. The company has raised its distribution by roughly 6% on average every year over the last 5 years and currently pays out a quarterly cash dividend of $0.43 per unit, kept unchanged from its prior distribution and $1.72 per unit annualized for a 5.5% dividend yield. I last covered BIP with a hold rating, highlighting the value of its deeply discounted investment-grade securities (BIPH) but also flagging possible risk from a widening of credit spreads if the U.S. were to fall into a recession. Critically, the odds of a US recession are plummeting. JPMorgan (JPM) initially placed this at 60% in April, dropping to 40% in May. Real GDP grew by 3% for the second quarter of 2025, comfortably beating expectations and likely pulling to near zero any lingering probability of a recession in 2025. This is typically defined as two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth. The improved economic backdrop drastically ramps up the likelihood of BIP achieving its 5% to 9% annual distribution growth target.

Broadly, a stronger global economic environment combined with an elevated inflation outlook sets the tone for renewed bullishness in BIP, and I've been buying shares in the C Corp in recent weeks. They have started to dip from recent highs, tracking the broader space for interest-rate-sensitive sectors lower as inflation looks to be heating up. BIP reported fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $5.43 billion, up 5.6% over its year-ago comp and a beat on consensus estimates. FFO came in at $638 million, around $0.81 per unit. This was up by 3 cents, around 5%, from its year-ago comp and a beat on consensus by $0.01 per unit. The company is targeting paying out roughly 60% to 70% of its FFO, hence, gains in FFO per unit directly translate to the capacity of BIP to raise its dividend in lieu of paying above its target payout ratio. This ratio stood at 68% as of the end of the second quarter, roughly unchanged from its year-ago comp.

Segment Performance And Preferreds

BIP's transport segment, which covers its rail and port businesses, as well as its toll roads, saw FFO dip by $15 million year-over-year as a result of capital recycling and the depreciation of the Brazilian real. BIP operates 9,800 km of rail in Brazil, of which 8,000 km is owned by the company. Adjusting for these would have placed FFO above the year-ago figure. There was a 3% increase in traffic at BIP's toll roads, which also experienced a 3% inflationary increase in rates.

Transport is BIP's largest segment by FFO, but its data segment is where the company is driving immense growth and focusing its investment efforts. FFO for the Data segment grew by a remarkable 45% year-over-year, driven by the bolt-on acquisition of 76,000 telecom tower sites in India and the ramping of additional megawatts across the company's global data center platform. BIP's data center business had 1.1 gigawatts of critical load capacity as of the end of the quarter and an additional 580 megawatts of contracted capacity. BIP also announced at the end of May that it would redeem its Toronto Stock Exchange-traded Series 1 Preferreds. The company's NYSE-listed preferreds; Series 13 (NYSE:BIP.PR.A) and Series 14 (NYSE:BIP.PR.B), will remain outstanding. These are currently trading hands for a discount to their $25 per share liquidation value of roughly 32.4% and 33.5%, respectively. And with a yield on cost of 7.5% available from both.

Liquidity And Economic Outlook

BIP has been such a strong performer over the long term because the company is able to capture organic growth from inflation-indexed revenues, positive GDP growth, and reinvested cash flows. These drivers of internal growth are backed by a diverse global infrastructure portfolio that has placed the company in a strong position as a pillar of my real assets portfolio. BIP portfolio is also extremely healthy, with an investment-grade rating of "BBB+" affirmed by Fitch Ratings a couple of weeks ago.

Total current liquidity as of the end of the second quarter stood at $5.7 billion, including $1.5 billion of cash across its businesses. I like that BIP's debt maturity schedule is incredibly well laddered with an average term of 8 years that is roughly 90% fixed for earnings visibility. The units have waned through July to reflect broader market angst over the direction of inflation. While BIP benefits from higher inflation due to indexation, the resulting increase in the Fed funds rate to fight inflation has driven up the cost of debt.

BIP held a net debt balance of $30.9 billion as of the end of its second quarter. A lowering of interest rates would benefit BIP, with the company driving significant price returns during the ZIRP ERA when it could borrow at low rates to invest in global infrastructure assets for a significant spread. The CME FedWatch Tool currently places the probability of a September rate cut at 39%, down from 73% at the start of July. We could see continued weakness to end 2025 if inflation continues to come in hot to further push back rate cuts. I'll continue to add to my position.

data.title: Walt Disney Q3 Earnings Preview: This Might Be The Inflection Point
data.description:
DIS Stock: FQ3 Earnings Schedule On August 6

I last covered Walt Disney Company (DIS) stock on May 22 in an article titled “The Walt Disney Company: Why Insiders Have Been Selling Around $110”. That article was promoted by the latest insider disclosures at that time and rated the stock as a hold on the following considerations:

I see a sharp divergence between the prevailing bullish sentiment surrounding The Walt Disney Company stock and its insider transactions. Investors should take the insiders’ activities into their investment decisions for several reasons. Their transactions were dominated by selling and were mostly reported in a narrow price range around $110. This is a price level that offers little margin of safety considering the headwinds reflected in its recent earnings report.

Since that analysis, there have been a few new developments surrounding the company and calls for a follow-up. Given that DIS is scheduled to release its FQ3 earnings report (ER, note that the company’s fiscal year 2025 ends on September 27th), I will integrate my analysis on these developments with my preview for its FQ3 ER. The overall takeaway is twofold. First, I am seeing large odds for continued earnings recovery in FQ3 and also full fiscal year 2025. Second, I will even go a step further and make an even stronger assertion about its FQ3 – this quarter might mark an inflection point for the company. For this reason, swing traders who want to bet on its FQ3 earnings surprise may find DIS stock timely.

To prime the subsequent discussion, the chart below provides a snap of Disney’s upcoming ER and also its previous ER. As seen, its FQ3 ER is scheduled for August 8, 2025, to be announced pre-market. The latest normalized EPS estimate from consensus for this quarter is projected at $1.45 on total revenue of $23.76 billion. These projections follow a strong performance in the latest quarter announced in May, where the company reported earnings results beating the estimates on all metrics.

Next, I will explain why DIS is well poised to beat consensus estimates again in FQ3 and also perform strongly for the full FY 2025.

DIS Stock: EPS Outlook And Catalysts

I see several EPS drivers for the media conglomerate in FQ3 and FY 2025. The top and most immediate driver on my list is the strong gains in its Entertainment and Experiences divisions. DIS has been investing heavily in its Experiences segment lately. Notably, Disney has announced plans to launch a new theme park in Abu Dhabi. Meanwhile, it has a large expansion program under way, both at its domestic parks and abroad. I won’t be surprised that it will be developing new lands, attractions, and strengthening its cruise lines too.

The second most important driver on my list is its streaming assets. After years of investment, its DTC operations were able to sustain a profit over the past few quarters. The company will be launching a new ESPN service, which should strengthen its sports and other content offerings. In the meantime, I expect Disney to continue making accreditive investment to further enhance the technology of its platforms and improve user experience. These moves should help widen the subscriber base and support continued DTC growth for the years to come.

With the aggressive investment in the above areas in recent years, the capital investment finally seems to have started decreasing. To wit, the chart below shows DIS’s quarterly Capital Expenditures. As seen, the company's CAPEX has been on an overall rising trend since its bottom in 2021 amid the COVID pandemic. After reaching a low point around $600M in 2020~2021, it has increased at a substantial pace and reached a recent peak of approximately $2.40B in FQ1 2025. After this temporary peak, FQ2 2025 ER reported a decline to $1.862B as seen. Looking ahead, I expect the ongoing restructuring measures to continue and the careful capital discipline to further bolster its profit margins.

DIS stock: Valuation

As a reflection of its earnings recovery potential, consensus estimates project its EPS to keep increasing at a good rate in the next few years. For example, consensus projects an EPS of $5.78 for FY 2025, translating into 16.2% annual growth rate, followed by another 2 years of solid growth at 9.3% and 12.1%. All told, consensus projections point to an EPS growth from $5.78 in FY 2025 to $7.05 by FY 2029, representing a 4% Compounded Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) for the next five years.

On the negative side, the stock’s EPS recovery potential is quite expensively priced, in my view. Based on the above projection, the stock’s forward FY1 P/E ratio is 20.62. With a growth rate in the range of 9.3% to 16.2% in the next 3 years, the PEG ratio is in the range between 1.3x and 2.2x, considerably above the ideal threshold of 1x.

Other valuation considerations show similar concerns, as reflected in its overall valuation grade of D in the chart below. Its FY1 P/E ratio of 20.6 is more than 48% above the sector median of 13.86, although it is more than 46% lower than its own 5-year average of 38.68. But investors need to bear in mind that its average P/E of 38.68 in the past 5 years can be biased towards the higher end for at least two reasons. First, the low accounting EPS during the COVID pandemic has resulted in high accounting P/E, which are outlier data points in my view. Second, the continued development of its streaming rivals has materially changed its competitive moat in the past 5 years in my assessment.

DIS Stock: Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Switching back to the near term, a few other immediate positives are worth noting for the FQ3 ER. Disney has enjoyed several strong theatrical slates in recent months (particularly during the summer months). As an example, “The Fantastic Four” just delivered a powerful opening and now sits at the top of the weekend gross list, as shown below. These hugely successful theatrical slates are only the beginning for DIS’s monetization mechanism, and they will help to boost profits at DIS’s other segments (theme parks, licensing, etc.) for years to come. As an example, the latest data show that DIS keeps being the most lucrative licensor, as detailed in the news report below.

Seeking Alpha News: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) continued its reign as it once again topped the list of global licensors in 2024, keeping its lead intact with an estimated $62 billion in retail sales of licensed products, according to the 2025 Top Global Licensors report. The sales of licensed products topped $300 billion in 2024 and were up ~10% from the prior year, according to the report.

To recap, this DIS reexamination is triggered by a few new developments since my last article, mainly its upcoming FQ3 ER, the latest development at its Entertainment and Experiences divisions, and the progress with its streaming assets. These developments have led me to see good odds for it to beat FQ3 estimates, making the stock interesting for swing traders who want to bet on earnings surprises.

data.title: Nordex: Maybe It Is Time To Take A Profit (Downgrade To Hold)
data.description:
Notes: Unless indicated otherwise, amounts in the article are in EUR. At the current exchange rate, one euro is approximately 1.14 U.S. dollars. All per-share figures refer to the NRDXF ticker and are in EUR.

Investment Thesis

Nordex Group SE (OTCPK:NRDXF, OTC:NRXXY) is a manufacturer of onshore wind turbines, headquartered in Hamburg, Germany. Nordex has an annual revenue of almost EUR 8 billion and over 57 GW of installed wind power capacity across more than 40 markets. Nordex has a strong focus on Europe, not a bad thing in times of constant tariff discussions. Europe accounted for 87% (in megawatts) of installations carried out in Q2 2025, Latin America for 10%, and the "Rest of the World" region for 2%.

I first covered Nordex here on Seeking Alpha in February and gave a buy recommendation (Nordex: Q4 Financial Results Could Be The Catalyst For A Turnaround). This has worked out fairly well. Shares are up almost 90% since then (in US dollars):

Nordex had not been profitable from 2019 to 2023, losing between EUR -72.6 million (2019) and EUR -497.8 million (2022) per year. However, I thought the company was making steady progress towards profitability. I expected Nordex to be profitable in Q4 and also the full FY 2024. I also forecasted positive FCF for the year. All that came to fruition, and subsequently, the stock has rallied.

Nordex announced its Q2 2025 financial results on July 28, and they were again strong. Key positives were an order intake of 2.3 GW, substantial YoY growth, and FCF of EUR 145 million.

Still, I am downgrading to Hold and have sold my position. There might be between 10% and 20% upside for the shares left, but I do not want to chase this. The German market seems overdue for a correction, and, in my view, chances are high that this will happen before the end of October. The DAX index was up 20% (on a total return basis) in 2023, 19% in 2024, and is up 21% YTD. The TecDAX, which includes Nordex, was up 15% in 2023, only 3% in 2024, and is up 10% YTD. However, the poor TecDAX performance in 2024 is distorted by a list of companies - like SMA Solar (OTCPK:SMTGF, OTCPK:SMTGY) or AIXTRON (OTCPK:AIXXF) (OTCPK:AIIXY) - that have retreated by more than 50%.

The risk-reward ratio no longer looks favorable to me. Of course, investors holding Nordex shares with a different entry point might see this differently. I am still convinced that long-term prospects for Nordex are positive, but I see some volatility in the short- to mid-term.

Let’s look at the Q2 20025 results.

Q2 2025 financial results

They were strong and above expectations, especially regarding profitability and free cash flow. The only disappointment was that sales were just EUR 1.87 billion, only a marginal YoY increase from EUR 1.86 billion in Q2 2024. Gross revenue, which includes changes in inventories, experienced a 3.1% rise, totaling EUR 1.9 billion, up from EUR 1.8 billion in the prior-year quarter. Nordex secured 2,310 MW of order intake in its Projects segment, an impressive 81.7% growth from the 1,271 MW recorded in Q2 2024. The service segment's order intake for Q2 2025 was EUR 438 million, slightly down from EUR 503.4 million in Q2 2024. At the end of the quarter, the total value of new orders, primarily from project business, amounted to EUR 2.2 billion in Q2 2025, a considerable increase from EUR 1.2 billion at the end of Q2 2024.

Profitability saw a notable improvement, with EBITDA increasing by 64.3% to EUR 108.2 million, compared to EUR 65.8 million in Q2 2024. This resulted in an improved EBITDA margin of 5.8%, up from 3.5% in Q2 2024. Similarly, EBIT for Q2 2025 was EUR 65.6 million, representing an EBIT margin of 3.5%. This marks a substantial YoY increase from EUR 22.3 million and a 1.2% EBIT margin in Q2 2024. Nordex SE also reported a strong net income of EUR 31 million in Q2 2025, a significant leap from EUR 0.5 million in the corresponding quarter last year.

Despite the significant profitability improvements, the share price rally in 2025 has made the stock quite expensive at the moment. As of July 2025, Nordex SE's trailing twelve-month P/E ratio is over 160. For the full fiscal year 2025, the estimated forward P/E ratio is around 30.

Balance sheet remains strong with a considerable net cash position

Nordex generated positive free cash flow of EUR 145.1 million, marking a significant increase of 54.4% compared to EUR 94.0 million in Q2 2024, primarily driven by its operational performance. This means the balance sheet remains strong, with a considerable net cash position. When I wrote my initial article, the net cash position was EUR 583 million, which came to 20.7% of the market cap. At the end of Q2 2025, the net cash position increased to EUR 942 million, which is now (even after the stock has rallied) still 18.5% of the market cap. Sooner or later, Nordex will have to use that cash for something, and that could be a positive future catalyst for the stock.

Nordex has reconfirmed its guidance for the full fiscal year 2025. The company anticipates consolidated sales to range between EUR 7.4 billion and EUR 7.9 billion (2024: EUR 7.3 billion), with an EBITDA margin expected to be between 5.0% and 7.0% (2024: 4.1%).

Risks

Key risks for Nordex are possible supply chain disruptions and a volatile political environment.

Nordex does little business in the US (but has growth aspirations). Therefore, neither tariffs nor Trump’s repeated tantrums against wind energy can do much harm.

As with the US, the risk from China and also from Chinese manufacturers is limited. Nordex does not operate in China. Chinese wind turbine manufacturers will probably not make significant progress in Europe in the short or mid-term (the long-term is a different story, though). The political climate is currently not supportive of Chinese manufacturers. The European Union has already launched an inquiry into Chinese suppliers of wind turbines under the Foreign Subsidies Regulation.

However, possibly shifting politics in Europe are a risk investors need to be aware of. In June, the French National Assembly, the lower house of parliament, voted on the adoption of a controversial amendment that proposed a moratorium on the development of new wind turbine and solar panel projects. While the bill was rejected with a significant majority, the debate underscores the ongoing political risks. Something similar is happening in the UK, where the Reform party explicitly states its intention to scrap the UK's net-zero target entirely. New orders in Q2 2025 were primarily driven by strong markets in Germany, Türkiye, and Latvia. Europe accounted for 87% (in megawatts) of new installations. Therefore, any negative developments in Europe regarding renewable energy will most likely affect Nordex.

Conclusion

Nordex reported Q2 2025 financial results that were above expectations. However, shares are up almost 90% since my buy recommendation in February, and they are starting to look expensive. In my view, odds are high that the German market will experience a correction in Q3, adding to the worsening risk/reward ratio. Therefore, I am downgrading Nordex to Hold and recommending that investors consider waiting for a lower entry point to start a new position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: Uncertainty Remains For Now, But The Patient Is Recovering
data.description:
Introduction

Here we are again, back at Medical Properties Trust (MPW). Since we are talking about one of the most turbulent REITs at this moment, frequent covering of the stock is important. Even though some of you would love to see me move on. Something that eventually will happen, but not yet!

So without further ado, I would like to share my thoughts on the Q2 2025 earnings release. It is important to understand that very soon, Medical Properties Trust will put out a new press release regarding the Prospect Medical Holdings chapter 11 bankruptcy process. I highly recommend catching up on this news. I've written about it a couple of days ago. To put it simply, Prospect is in the process of selling its operations in MPW's hospitals/behavioral facilities. Since no Prospect-related revenue is currently being reported in MPW's numbers, I believe that resolving this issue in the near future will be a net-positive going forward from a cash flow perspective. But we are talking about Medical Properties Trust, so we have to see for ourselves first.

In the mean time, I want to share with you my take on the Q2 2025 report. So let's begin.

The Q2 2025 Report

First I want to walk you through all the important quarter-over-quarter changes, to see how the cash flows from operations are trending thus far. First of all, we see no change in normalized funds from operations per share. This is a preferable starting point for REIT. It normalizes the numbers for one-time events that would otherwise skew the underlying performance of a REIT.

Regarding the share-based compensation. Since management is heavily compensated in shares, volatility in share price can lead to volatility in the reported earnings. When you take the total share-based compensation expense using grant date fair value for all awards (including cash-settled awards), you arrive at $18.5 million for the first six months ended June 30, 2025. I mention this because, in my humble opinion, management is overpaid when you look at the negative value creation that long-term investors have experienced in recent years. But it's important to keep a cool mind. In the medium term, there are good reasons to trust management. Let me explain...

In my article that was published after the Q1 2025 report, I highlighted the fact that the management compensation plan has changed. I share the most important part below. What you see is that management has an incentive to reach a share price of $7. When the stock hits that price level, management will receive a lot of their stock-based compensation. Because – obviously – we as shareholders will also benefit from this market value per share, I have a strong case to at least stick to (the bulk) of my shares until that price level is reached.

But let's dive a bit deeper into the stagnant Normalized Funds From Operations [NFFO] during Q2 2025. Because there is a valid reason why this number didn't change this quarter. Before I get to the most important line items, let me first touch on the property-related and general and administrative expenses. The property-related expenses relate to ground leases and other expenses (taxes and insurance, to name a few) that MPW pays directly. The total amount is reimbursed by the tenants. So this number won't affect MPW, only from a short-term liquidity standpoint. Regarding the G&A expenses, the biggest contributor to the decline in these line items is because of fair-value adjustments. All of which is already incorporated in the NFFO numbers that I touched on before.

What we are getting is (finally) some top-line growth in the rent-billed line item. Growing by a very meaningful 7.3% quarter-over-quarter. From this +/- $12 million in growth, around $7.6 million is coming from new tenants that took over Steward post-bankruptcy. The rest should probably be attributed to rent escalators.

The fact that NFFO didn't grow in Q2, despite a rapid increase in 'rent billed' is because of the enormous $2.5 billion refinancing effort that took place this year. Impacting interest expenses quite a bit. But those interest expenses are not increasing next quarter, revenue is...

So if new tenants keep ramping up payments and future tenants that take on operations of Prospect Medical Group come in, (Ceteris Paribus), we should see a pretty meaningful increase in both the top-line as well as the bottom-line. In this bullish scenario, the stock will definitely creep back up.

I want to end this part of the article with the dividend coverage. Indeed, dividends are not covered by free cash flow. Subtracting non-cash revenue (straight-line rent) from NFFO will get you to $0.07 per share ($0.14 - $0.07). And this excludes any capital expenditures that go towards new (small) equity investments, like the one in Infracore (around $61 million dollars at today's exchange rate). Meaning that management is expecting dividends to be covered very soon. Coming from new tenants ramping up payments and the Prospect resolution. You can argue about management's decision to keep paying a dividend at a negative payout ratio, but since MPW is not planning to expand its portfolio or something, you could argue that it is a logical move to keep paying a dividend. Since (Ceteris Paribus) the dividend will be covered by free cash flow rather sooner than later.

MPW's Debt Picture (And The JV's)

Due to the impact of fair value adjustments (for example) and the limited transparency that Joint Ventures (JVs) have, it's difficult to draw a precise picture of all that is happening. Instead, I try to identify broad trends rather than pin down exact figures.

When we take a look at the (non-covenant) debt metrics, we can at least see that MPW's financial leverage (total debt as a percentage of total gross assets) is more or less the same.

It's important to see how this financial leverage will develop in Q3, especially since the Prospect bankruptcy process is currently about to wrap up.

But perhaps even more important are the Joint Ventures. Let's calculate the financial leverage on these JV's. Especially how this metric is trending. The relevance of doing this is related to the fact that MPW's management has increased its equity investment in the Infracore JV this quarter. And no matter the reason behind it, I believe that management has lost so much trust that any investment they make feeds into the negativity. So let's take a look.

In order to compare MPW's own leverage to the JV's leverage, we need to calculate all of this using the same formula. In calculating its financial leverage, Medical Properties Trust defines total gross assets as follows:

When we take a look at the JV's, we get the following financial leverage:

  • Q1 2025: $1,070,938 / ($2,462,449 + $320,045) x 100% = 38.5%
  • Q2 2025: $1,171,760 / ($2,784,268 + $367,250) x 100% = 37.1%

As you can see, the JV's leverage is lower and decreasing. These are positive signs.

Not only are the Joint Ventures more conservatively leveraged, the leverage is also declining. But as we have seen with both Steward and Prospect, a lot of investments were not retrieved when both these tenants went belly up. So just as important is the operational performance of the JV's.

I focus mainly on operational costs: property-related, interest, and G&A. Since these are - more or less - cash expenses that strongly correlate with operational performance. When we take those three line items as a percentage of total revenue, we get the following trend:

  • Q1 2025: ($2,057 + $12,136 + $613) / $38,512 x 100% = 38.4%
  • Q2 2025: ($2,366 + $13,200 + $807) / $42,704 x 100% = 38.3%

Again we see a declining percentage of revenue going towards expenses.

So, from the Joint Ventures front (at least on the surface), I don't see deteriorating fundamentals; both from a leverage and an operational performance perspective.

Excluding Infracore (which owns and leases 18 Switzerland facilities, excluding two facilities currently under development), all of the underlying JV assets are fully leased. Yes, that equals to 100%. Since we have no occupancy number related to Infracore, the following part of the conference call might be worth mentioning. A 21% year-over-year revenue growth seems very good.

How are MPW's new tenants really doing?

Sure, revenue is ramping up, so we could say that tenants that replaced Steward are doing fine. But a lot of more skeptical investors have pointed out that these new operators are – on average – low-tier operators. So let's start by taking a look at what MPW's management had to say:

From what I can see (compared to Q1 2025 --> chapter: How are [new] tenants doing?) things are currently trending nicely...? Even College Health and Tenor Health (both not mentioned in the CC) seem to be on track:

When looking at the financial support that Sharon Regional Medical Center (or Tenor Health, who is now operating it) received, we can see real evidence that hospitals are critical real estate infrastructure where U.S. citizens rely on. Sure, we should not forget the predatory practices of PE-firms (like Steward) that financially drained those hospitals for personal gain. But there was COVID, a huge spike in labor costs and overall inflation that shut down capital markets and ballooned interest expenses. Not even talking about medical reimbursements that need time to catch up. It was a perfect storm that management couldn't foresee. What we can do is look at the current trends regarding company performance. And as far as I can see right now: Medical Properties Trust seems to be recovering.

Risks (And Opportunities)

Medical Properties has been a really bad investment for many investors. Luckily I was late to the party to buy into this REIT when all the screaming buys were flying around. I started a small position at $12 and bought my last batch around the $3 range. My average purchase price sits around $6.35. So I am still sitting on a loss (including dividend payments) like many of you.

For now, I am here to keep a very close eye on all (public) developments that are currently unfolding before our eyes. Especially the Prospect story will be very interesting to follow in the coming week.

The sale and leaseback business approach that Medical Properties Trust relied on for years, has attracted predatory PE firms that drained hospitals and left after nothing was left anymore. This systemic problem can only be cured by robust legislation. Until then, REITs that still rely on this structure will trade at discounted fair values going forward. But at the current $4.05 price, the stock is still being punished beyond what I think is reasonable. Even at the depressed Q2 2025 NFFO ($0.14), the annualized P/NFFO multiple is 7.1x. The average multiple comes in at 11.43x. Pre-COVID, the multiple even reached higher than 16x. But those were different times, so you should never expect MPW to go back to those levels anytime soon. But 7.1x at these depressed levels while at the same time we see early signs of recovery? I'm not convinced that +/- $4 a share is reasonable.

Medical Properties Trust seems to be in a turnaround phase. Risks associated with the Prospect chapter 11 outcome are real and unknown for now. So it is hard to speculate on that. Before August 11th, we should learn more regarding this chapter 11 development. Until then, I don't see a clear reason to panic. At a very low P/NFFO multiple that is about to grow rapidly if new tenants keep ramping up payments and post-Prospect tenants are taking over operations, the upside potential could still be huge. And even if something goes wrong, cutting the dividend will still prevent Medical Properties Trust to go under.

I want to close this chapter by saying that shorting Medical Properties Trust is dangerous. Yes, you could say that management are all bandits that are here to ruin this company. Or that an enormous amount of hospitals will go bankrupt (where do those patients go, by the way?) But let's be fair, this is pure and unproven speculation. At the current valuation, there is no logical reason to short Medical Properties Trust, based on fundamental trends and the overall outlook. You can be more bearish or skeptical, but going short on this company is another story. The upside is limited (if the stock goes to $0) but the downside is limitless.

This is not going to be your new Enron or Wirecard. So even though the shorts could be right theoretically (management are crooks that have manipulated the numbers for years), the odds are much higher that fundamentals keep improving than that things are starting to fall apart again. If you want to gamble, go to a casino. You have a higher change of winning there than you have in shorting Medical Properties Trust. With a 35.5% of the float shorted (as of June 30th) it is becoming really risky...

Closing Remarks

I will probably be here until the share price reaches $7. Since I don't trust MPW's management (there is no recent track record to do so), selling (most) of my position when management gets rewarded themselves, makes a lot of sense to me. Until then, my core focus is on the trends and developments regarding portfolio performance and tenant health (including Prospect's unfolding endgame).

I started covering Medical Properties Trust back in December last year. Calculating the intrinsic value using REIT specific metrics. If I see a steady operational trend in the coming quarters, I will go back to my fundamental valuation calculations. But for now, it's way more useful to myself and you fellow investors to focus on more important metrics that will influence the stock price: portfolio stability and tenants that pay rent.

Thanks for stepping by. I appreciate it.

data.title: Got Summer Plans? Canadians Are Keeping It Local And Boosting Small Businesses
data.description:
With summer travel in high gear, Canadians have been hitting the road. But this year many have been choosing to spend a lot of their travel dollars close to home. Julia Kelly, Vice President of Small Business Banking at TD, discusses a recent TD survey showing how Canadians are buying into domestic tourism and supporting small Canadian businesses in the process.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - With summer travel season in high gear, Canadians are hitting the road and choosing to spend a lot of their travel dollars closer to home. A recent TD survey shows Canadians are buying into domestic tourism and supporting small Canadian business in the process. Here to talk us through the trends that she's seeing is Julia Kelly, Vice President of Small Business Banking at TD. So great to have you with us, Julia.

Julia Kelly - Thank you. Thank you for having me.

Greg Bonnell - Let's start with the big picture. We do have a lot of economic uncertainty. We've been living with it for a while now. But it's summertime. Canadians still want to travel. What stood out the most for you in these findings?

Julia Kelly - You know, it's terrific news for small business owners across Canada because we learned nearly 9 in 10 Canadians said that supporting small businesses was important to them. Canadians have not put their travel plans on hold, with 60% of Canadians planning to travel within Canada this summer.

Greg Bonnell - Now, I understand the findings, too, and this is interesting to me because usually people say, I got vacation plans. It's a big location. I'm going to go -- I went to Banff earlier this year. Some say, I'm going to the east coast. I'm going to go to Quebec City.

Canadians, nearly 3/4 of them saying they're willing to travel just to visit a unique local business, whether it's a restaurant, a shop, a quirky attraction. What does that tell you about the consumer and their behavior right now?

Julia Kelly - Well, it's really a shift in traveling domestically. 64% of Canadians are planning to travel within Canada over the summer months. And there's been a keen interest in supporting local Canadian businesses. 9 in 10 have said it's important to them to support local. And keeping in mind, summer travel doesn't always mean traveling across the country. Sometimes it might be staying pretty close to home, traveling in their home province.

And while visiting unique businesses is important to them, what is evident in the trend is that when planning a trip, they're making a point to research, whether that's through social media or through different outlets and community advertisements, visiting local attractions like restaurants who have a specific or unique flavor or businesses that are really specialized to that local area. People are looking for a unique experience, something they can't get anywhere else.

Greg Bonnell - So here is opportunity for these small businesses. Also, imagine some challenges. How would this -- I'm thinking of a seasonal community, a rural community start to tap into this demand for this experience to grow their reach?

Julia Kelly - So we know 73% of Canadians will consider a travel destination based on this unique attraction they might have. And so, really, it's about staying true to what you do well as a unique business in a local economy. So when you think about that, it might be - what is specific to that area? What might be something that is grown or unique or not able to get anywhere else, especially outside of a city? And something that might be a bit quieter that is an attraction for a summer plan and you can't get during the winter months.

Greg Bonnell - I was telling you before we came on air that in the spring, I went to Banff. It was my 25th wedding anniversary. Despite the economic uncertainty, I had to go big because it was my 25th wedding anniversary. But we do have this uncertainty, people keeping a little tighter hold on their money. Is this shaping the way Canadians are choosing to travel, choosing to spend?

Julia Kelly - You know, the uncertainty has been trending for a while, and we've heard from a lot of small business owners regarding concern around consumer spending and slowing down of spending. So learning that tourism will pick up across the country has really been welcome news, especially when we look at small businesses that are subject to seasonality. And the summer months, with increase in local traffic and this initiative to support local businesses, will carry not just through the summer months but even into the fall. So it's exactly what we wanted to hear - that Canadians are looking to support local small businesses as part of their summer vacation plans.

Greg Bonnell - So when you speak to these small businesses across the country and the owners, which I know you do, what are they telling you about this environment and how they're adapting to it?

Julia Kelly - Small business owners are one thing. They are resilient. And I think there are Canadians who have really shown how important it is to them to support these local small businesses. So as we've seen through the survey results, we know that there is uncertainty, and they're taking time to think intentionally on how to mitigate the impacts of uncertainty, adapt their businesses to attract more customers, and work with local suppliers to understand how they can really impact their bottom line, especially if they're seeing an influx of local travelers.

Greg Bonnell - In Canada, of course, summer has all too short a life. We head into the fall and the winter. Perhaps some of these businesses won't see the kind of support just simply because we're back at work and we're not traveling as much. What kind of support or advice do you think some of these businesses will need to keep thriving and evolving in this new landscape?

Julia Kelly - I think it's important for small businesses to really understand - get in touch with your advisor. At TD, we keep the dialogue open with our clients. We want small business customers to succeed, and I encourage customers to meet with your small business advisor to have those discussions around cash flow management. What are their options? How are they thinking about advice when they are faced with uncertainty? And together, we can proactively find solutions. And so keeping that dialogue open is really critical as they look forward into an uncertain landscape.
